title: Why Writing Your Book Alone Is Holding You Back: The Power of Book Midwives
guestName: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski
guestBio: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski are the co-founders of Synergy Publishing Group and creators of the Rebel Writers program. These 'book midwives' combine decades of writing expertise with a revolutionary approach to helping mission-driven women birth their books through community, embodiment, and retreat experiences.
excerpt: What if the secret to finally writing your book isn't more discipline or better time management, but community and embodied writing? Aimee sits down with her own 'book midwives' to reveal why women write differently and how to honor your unique creative process.
metaDescription: Discover why writing your book alone is holding you back. Learn the embodied approach to authorship that's changing how women write and publish their stories.
keyTakeaways: Writing is an embodied act that requires community, nourishment, and permission to be messy.
The defining question for any manuscript is: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?'
Women write differently than men and need to honor their unique creative processes rather than forcing traditional methods.
Your body knows your story before your mind does, so listen to what wants to emerge.
There's never a perfect time to write a book, just like having a baby or getting a puppy.
Legacy authors don't just write one book—they step into writing as part of their identity.
The people you least expect will be most transformed by your words, so don't hold back your story.

pullQuotes.text: What absolutely has to be in this book? That question changed everything for my writing process.
Your body knows your story before your mind does—we just need to create space to listen.
Writing doesn't happen in a vacuum. You need community, feedback, and care to birth something meaningful.

timestamps.label: Why Writing is an Embodied Act

content:
If you've been telling yourself that you just need more discipline to write your book, more time blocked on your calendar, or a better writing app, I'm about to challenge everything you think you know about the writing process. Because what if the best way to write your book is to never do it alone?

In this deeply personal episode, I sit down with Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski, the incredible women I call my book midwives. These are the founders of Synergy Publishing Group who didn't just help me write The Manage Her book—they completely transformed how I think about authorship, creativity, and the messy magic of bringing ideas into the world.

Writing as an Embodied Act

One of the most revolutionary concepts Shana and Cindy introduced me to is that writing isn't just a mental exercise—it's an embodied act. Your body holds your stories, your wisdom, your experiences in ways your logical mind hasn't even processed yet. This is why so many of us get stuck when we try to force our writing into rigid structures or follow someone else's blueprint for success.

Cindy explains it beautifully: your body knows your story before your mind does. We just need to create the space, the safety, and the community to let what wants to emerge actually come through. This is why their retreat experience at Synergy House includes yoga, nourishing meals, and spreading manuscript pages across every surface of their North Carolina property. Writing becomes a full-body, full-life experience rather than something you squeeze into the margins of an already overwhelming schedule.

This embodied approach completely changed my relationship with my own book. Instead of sitting at my computer trying to force words onto a blank page, I learned to trust the process, to let ideas flow in their own time, and to honor the way my creativity actually works rather than fighting against it.

The Question That Changes Everything

Here's the question that transformed my entire manuscript: What absolutely has to be in this book? Not what you think people want to hear, not what other business books include, not what feels safe or polished. What absolutely, without question, has to be in this book?

This question cuts through all the noise, all the second-guessing, all the imposter syndrome that keeps so many brilliant women from ever finishing their manuscripts. It forces you to get clear on your unique message, your specific wisdom, and the transformation you're here to create in the world.

For me, answering this question meant getting vulnerable in ways I hadn't expected. It meant including stories I wasn't sure I was ready to share, frameworks I was still developing, and truths that felt uncomfortably raw. But that's exactly what made the book authentic, powerful, and genuinely helpful to the women who needed to hear it.

How Women Write Differently

One of the most validating parts of working with Shana and Cindy was learning that women often write differently than men—and that's not a problem to solve, it's a strength to honor. Women tend to write in circles rather than straight lines, weaving stories together, making connections that might not seem logical at first but create deeper meaning and resonance.

We also tend to need more community, more processing time, more space to explore ideas out loud before committing them to paper. The traditional image of the solitary author locked away in a cabin might work for some people, but it doesn't serve most of the women they work with. Instead, their Rebel Writers program creates community around the writing process—women supporting women, sharing feedback, holding space for each other's creative emergence.

If you've been struggling with traditional writing advice, if you've started and stopped your book multiple times, if you need to talk through your ideas before writing them down, you're not broken. You're just honoring your own creative process, and it's time to find support systems that work with your nature rather than against it.

The Retreat Experience That Changes Everything

Synergy House isn't just a pretty location for writing retreats—it's a carefully crafted container for transformation. When women arrive, they're invited to spread their work across dining room tables, living room floors, and covered porches. They eat meals together, practice yoga together, and hold space for each other's creative breakthroughs and emotional releases.

This isn't about churning out chapters on a deadline. It's about creating the conditions where your best work can emerge naturally. Sometimes that means writing for hours in a flow state. Sometimes it means taking a walk to process a difficult chapter. Sometimes it means crying through a story you didn't know you needed to tell.

The retreat model recognizes that writing a book is often a spiritual and emotional journey as much as it is a practical one. You're not just organizing information—you're excavating wisdom, processing experiences, and stepping into a new identity as someone who has something important to say to the world.

Becoming a Legacy Author

Perhaps the most powerful shift Shana and Cindy offer is the concept of legacy authorship. This isn't about writing one book and checking it off your list. It's about stepping into writing as part of who you are, as a key way you contribute to the world and build the legacy you want to leave.

Legacy authors understand that their first book is just the beginning. They build creative partnerships, develop their voice over time, and see each project as part of a larger body of work that reflects their evolution as leaders, mothers, entrepreneurs, and women.

This long-term perspective takes so much pressure off any individual project. Your book doesn't have to be perfect or contain everything you'll ever want to say. It just needs to contain what wants to emerge right now, what absolutely has to be shared at this moment in your journey.

If you've been waiting for the perfect time to write your book, if you've been trying to force yourself through a process that doesn't fit how you naturally create, or if you've been wondering whether your story even matters, I want you to hear this: there's never a perfect time, your process is valid exactly as it is, and the people who most need your words are waiting for you to find the courage to share them.

The question isn't whether you have a book in you. The question is whether you're ready to find the community, support, and embodied approach that will help you birth it into the world.

quiz.title: What's Your Creative Expression Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally bring your ideas to life and what might be holding you back from sharing your story with the world.

questions.question: When you have a creative project you're excited about, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I need to feel into it first and let the idea marinate before I take action
I start researching the best methods and create a detailed plan
I immediately think about who I can brainstorm with or get feedback from
I envision the full impact this could have and start mapping out the bigger picture

questions.question: What tends to derail your creative momentum most often?

options.text: Trying to force myself to work when my body and energy aren't aligned
Getting stuck in planning mode and never feeling ready to start the messy work
Worrying that I don't have enough support or that I'm bothering people by asking for help
Getting overwhelmed by the scope of what I want to create

questions.question: When you think about sharing your story or expertise, what comes up for you?

options.text: I have so much to say but it doesn't always come out in a logical order
I need to organize my thoughts better before anyone would want to hear them
I wonder if anyone would actually be interested or if I'm qualified enough
I see how it could change everything, but the responsibility feels huge
Natural light, comfortable seating, and the freedom to move around or change locations
A dedicated workspace with all my tools organized and a clear schedule
Other people nearby, even if we're working on different things, or regular check-ins with collaborators
Inspiring visuals and reminders of the bigger mission driving this work

questions.question: What's your relationship with 'perfect timing' for big creative projects?

options.text: I believe in divine timing and trust that things will happen when they're meant to
I keep waiting for the right time when I have all the pieces in place
I'd feel more confident starting if I knew others were on the journey with me
I know there's never a perfect time, but I want to make sure the impact will be worth it

questions.question: When you imagine your creative work making an impact, what matters most to you?

options.text: That it feels authentic and true to who I really am
That it's well-crafted and provides real value to people
That it creates connection and helps people feel less alone
That it transforms how people think or creates lasting change

results.title: The Intuitive Creator
results.description: You trust your gut and know that your best ideas come when you're not forcing them. You understand that creativity is a full-body experience and you honor the messy, non-linear process of bringing something meaningful into the world. Your challenge is often finding the right community and structure to support your natural flow. This episode will show you how to embrace your embodied creative process and find the support system that honors how you actually work best.

results.title: The Structured Storyteller
results.description: You thrive with frameworks and clear processes, but sometimes wonder if you're missing something deeper in your creative work. You have incredible stories and wisdom to share, but you might be trying to force them into systems that don't quite fit. Your organized approach is a strength, but your stories deserve to breathe too. This episode will help you discover how to blend your love of structure with a more intuitive, embodied approach to creativity.

results.title: The Community Builder
results.description: You know instinctively that the best work happens when we're not alone, and you naturally create spaces for others to shine. You understand the power of collaboration and support, but you might hold back from fully stepping into your own story. Your gift for bringing people together is exactly what the world needs. This episode will inspire you to see how your community-focused nature can become the foundation for sharing your own powerful message.

results.title: The Legacy Visionary
results.description: You think big and see the long-term impact of the work you want to create in the world. You're not interested in one-time projects but in building something that will matter for years to come. Sometimes this big vision can feel overwhelming or paralyzing. Your desire to create lasting change is your superpower. This episode will show you how to step into your identity as someone who creates meaningful legacy work, starting with trusting what wants to emerge through you right now.

transcript:
**Aimee:** If you've ever dreamed of writing a book, this episode is for you. Today on The Manager, I'm sitting down with the two women who helped me bring my book to life, Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski, founders of Synergy Publishing Group, my publishers, my creative partners, and the true book midwives of The Manager.

Welcome back to The Manager podcast, the show where we turn invisible work into visible leadership. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus. Working with Shana and Cindy was one of the most magical experiences of my life. I flew to a lake house in North Carolina, and we spent the entire weekend eating, talking, doing yoga, laying in the sun, and spreading printed pages across every surface. And then they asked me the question that changed everything. Aimee, what absolutely has to be in this book? That one moment anchored the entire manuscript.

Since then, Synergy has grown in extraordinary ways. From purchasing their own retreat property, the Synergy House, to launching their powerful new program, Rebel Writers, designed for women creating from fierce alignment. And because they're writers themselves, their coaching hits differently. Shana's new book, Shit No One Tells You About Starting an Online Business, released on January 11th of 2026. And Cindy's new book, Your Aligned Girl in a Crisis, A Practice in Not Borrowing Trouble, arrives on February 6th.

You'll also get to meet them both in person at the Manager Conference in Las Vegas on March 13th through the 15th, 2026, where they'll be joining me as panelists and breakout session leaders. These women don't just publish books. They birth movements, legacies, and voices the world needs now. So let's dive into the truth, the embodiment, and the fiercely aligned creation behind writing transformational books. Ladies, thank you so much for being here today. Shana, Cindy, you guys are awesome.

So I wanted to start back at my beginning, I guess. So I wanted to start with our retreat in North Carolina. Can we walk the listeners through that weekend, the food, the yoga, the pages everywhere, and why this intuitive process works so powerfully for authors?

**Shana:** Oh, man. It's fun to think about just that particular retreat, but Cindy and I's history with the vibe and the way that we approach a moment and experience like that goes, oh gosh, are we going 20 years plus back now at this point, which is awesome and amazing and doesn't make us feel old at all.

But Cindy and I both have always from our K through 12 teaching days to our university professor days to our now supporting amazing women like you days, writing is an embodied act and it sometimes needs to be in community and presence with others and so that retreat to get into our body using Cindy's amazing yoga experience as well as her amazing nourishment that she provides and then just to be connected with others who are struggling and figuring it out and letting it be messy with their words at the same time. That's where lots of fun magic can happen.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Lots of fun magic. I was just so surprised. And for me, it was this stark contrast between a publishing house in New York City where you're in some tall building in some office with some dude. And instead, we're at a retreat house at a lake house, sleeping in the same house, eating food and just communing together the way that we were doing work in the way that women do work. And I recognized it and I saw it right away and it was so aligned. And so such a big part of my mission is just to remind women that it's okay to do things the way that we do things.

And I think you said it best last night, Aimee, when we were on a call and you said something about my hair being longer and I said, no, it's just not in a ponytail because that's the way we work. That's the way women work. We pull it out of our faces. At that retreat, we were in yoga clothes or pajamas most of the time. And we were making serious, serious words happen on the page to influence the rest of the world. It's just amazing to see the way that women are transforming the way that they work and honoring themselves in such intuitive ways. And I absolutely see you guys for doing that.

Thank you for doing that. You two are true book midwives. I say that because I'm a home birther and I'm like, I couldn't birth without my midwife and I couldn't have created this book without you guys. So from my personal experience, I just, I'm so grateful for the way that you guys are able to become a home for women who are writing their truth and their embodiment and their legacy. How did you guys get here as Synergy Publishing Group?

**Shana:** Well, as you said, it goes way back and we were writers and teachers together first. Cindy and I met at the Summer Institute for the National Writing Project, which was the start of my PhD program that Cindy then also wound up doing very shortly behind me. But Cindy was leading that particular course and experience and I always think about the book we then wrote together with some other teacher writer friends. And one of the stories we told, which is fundamental to everything we do, we think we're all storytellers. I think everyone's a storyteller in some way, shape or form and have stories to pass along and share.

But we had kind of our initial bonding moment over a day book, which is cow covered neat composition notebook kindergartners used. And we've used, still use to this day, some version of that. And I was at the time teaching full time, going to school full time and wanting to be as efficient as possible. And so here they come with this gobbledygook in my mind at that time with glue sticks and we're putting things in and it's messy. And I'm like, listen, I got a PhD to get to. Can I just be on my computer? And Cindy goes, you're cute. Why don't you just give it a whirl? Let's just see what happens.

Well, many books later, many presentations and workshops and writing opportunities, and now working together as partners with other people and their words, obviously there was some kind of, okay, I trust Cindy, Cindy trusts me, and kind of the rest has been history. But we've done many things over the years together, but we really got together around helping people with their books in 2021. And that's where kind of my business, my coaching pivoted to why am I not helping people with their writing? Cindy was the first person I thought of to kind of connect with and, yeah. What happened next?

**Cindy:** Yeah. Well, I would be remiss if I didn't say the first book we wrote together was about day books that Shana didn't want to use and how to use them in the classroom. And we bonded again because we both had something going on with our hips. I was a runner and Shana was a college soccer athlete and we could not walk or sit in a chair. So we wrote that book laying on the floor, in a university office laying on our bellies and Shana introduced me to yoga. That was a long, long, long time ago.

And then we got here because we are passionate, have always been passionate about people's words. And something we believe deeply is that when we ask people to write, whether they be our students or someone we have just bumped into who has a story to tell, we're asking them to put their insides on the outside. And we feel deeply honored that people will share their insides with us and trust us with those insides and the way forward. And I think it's that core value of being in our bodies on that floor with our hips, trying to write that book and that connection there all the way to we're going to have a yoga and writing retreat. We're going to do some yoga. We're going to do some writing. We're going to wiggle. It's going to be messy. We're going to spread papers everywhere. I just invite people to make the biggest mess they possibly can in all of their practices.

**Aimee:** Which is like birth. That's just like birth.

**Cindy:** Just like birth. It's a mess. And then you get something beautiful. It's a mess and you're taking over the living room. That was what we were doing. We were taking over the living room just like when you do when you're giving birth at home. It really was. It was the home birth of a book.

**Shana:** Yeah. Just like that. And so you feel it in your body. You make a big mess. You need nourishment along the way. And all of those things come together into what we do.

**Aimee:** Just beautiful, you guys. One defining moment of the retreat was when you guys asked me, I think it was you, Shana, you're like, you looked at me and you're like, what absolutely has to be in this book? And why is that so important for the author to make that decision?

**Shana:** Most of the people we come in contact with, I say this in jest, but they want to save the world or sliver of it, right? In some way, shape or form, they are mission driven people, purpose driven people, thought leaders, no matter how tiny their connections or communities are or how large they are, everyone we come into contact with, that's the common thread is the things that they may be sharing about their life and career experiences, their business strategies, their mother-in-laws, their relationships, their poetry, whatever it is, it all has some feel and connection to humanity and I want to have some kind of ripple effect in that. That's the greatest gift that we hear everyone wanting.

When you embark on something like a book that's only going between this cover and this cover, to save the world in one said book is quite difficult. And so we excavate as we go. And so you were ready. You had lots and lots and lots and lots of words. And we love that. And that's great. That's what we want people to do versus going in and trying to think we need to know what we want to write before we put the first word down. That's our biggest myth that we love to bust when people start to work with us.

So at that point it was, okay, we have all of this amazing stuff and it may belong in a book, but does it belong in this book? And so that classic Stephen King, time to kill your darlings. Yes, you wrote it and that was good. And we're glad you did. And it was for a reason. And does this absolutely have to be in here? Is it absolutely a part of the core message, the core thing you want the reader to walk away with? You were supportive and you were like, okay, I have a direction. And so we could more easily say, nope, we're going to save this. And now some things are going into book two, right? And so we don't get rid of anything officially. But it's just a nice way to kind of trim the fat and say, all right, what does this really want to be? You've breathed life into this thing and now it's ready to tell you in relationship with you what it wants to be. So I think that question just kind of helps everybody.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it was major. I was like, oh, what do you mean we can't keep it all, Shana? I was like, OK, if I have to pick, OK, let me pick. Let me pick my absolute favorite parts. What's the absolute most important thing right now?

So you guys, Synergy has evolved so much since 2025, especially with the Synergy house. What inspired you guys to create a physical home for writers?

**Cindy:** I would say, well, it started because we got tired of hunting Airbnb, looking for the perfect writing retreat. And we were like, why don't we just build one? So we started there and then, my husband and I may have gotten up to some trespassing on the property that we now currently own when we saw it online. And it was, we keep using the word magic, but there is something truly magic about this place. We call it the vortex. People get sucked into the vortex because it's heavily wooded. It's on a land preserved so we cannot have neighbors there, which seems like we wouldn't want that. But we have so much wildlife, critters. We have chipmunks. We have our own personal chipmunks. We have our own personal deer. We're going to put up wood duck boxes. So there's woods everywhere. It doesn't have a resort feel. It has a back to nature feel, which is what we love and what we love the most about different places we had used before. So the inspiration came from the places we had seen in the places we had been, and we wanted to capture that experience and be able to offer more of it in a way that felt fiercely aligned for us.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it's really cool. I remember when you guys announced it to all of us, you know, who was basically like, hey, we've got this house now and we're going to use it and then you guys get to use it too. And I was like, wait, what? Come and throw your retreats. You have a space to do that now. And that's pretty amazing, really, honestly.

I think it's another example of kind of just how we like to move and have learned that feels really good for us to move and what feels good often to other women as well is letting those ideas, which feels really random. We're staying in these various retreat homes for our retreats. And we're like, wonder if we could have one of these one day. And Cindy and I dreamed and schemed, we looked at the really, really all over, Norman is a pretty big Lake in North Carolina, just North of Charlotte and so much development, so many houses that are gorgeous and beautiful and pristine.

**Shana:** And, it was just something where we were like, okay, okay. We see that. We see that. We see that. And then we started looking at land and we got curious and we just kept getting curious. We had zero idea of how, I think that's the biggest thing. Just like when you're writing, we had no idea because no one was sitting there with, necessarily the resources, the time and energy to figure it out. We just were like, this keeps showing up for us. We're going to trust that it maybe is meant to be. We don't know how it will show up. And yeah, literally, we started really, really talking about it and looking intentionally, more intentionally in 2024. And then we found this property as Cindy and her husband. January 2025.

**Cindy:** January 2025. And the interesting thing that, you know, we could say is random, but we know it's not, is we did not have a retreat planned for this year. We had one last year that you attended. And we said, what if we wait and not have our next retreat until February of 2026 to give space and feel no pressure for us to, because planning your retreat is a big thing, and see where a house might want to show up, that becomes ours. And we let February 2026 be our first retreat in our retreat house. And here we are. It was so interesting that we took the pressure off of us to give space to it. And it's happened exactly as we said.

**Aimee:** It's amazing. And I'll be there in February for the retreat.

**Cindy:** Yay.

**Aimee:** To finish book number two, because this is how we do it.

**Cindy:** This is how we do it. It's amazing. It's amazing. I'm so proud of you guys.

So you talk about being fiercely aligned, only doing what matches your integrity. How has that shaped Synergy House and the Rebel Writers program?

**Shana:** Rebel Writers and Synergy House both came from, we really also want to impact and believe in the power of words. And I truly think we are all messaging beings. I think everyone has something to say, share, write, speak, sing, draw, paint. I think we all have that in us, whether we believe we do is a whole other thing. And in the K-12 and college classrooms, Cindy and I were on a mission that those humans that walk through our door, that's really what we were trying to do from day one to whatever the last day of class was, is to help them believe that they had words that what they wanted to express was important.

And so the people, we don't have that fight as much with the people who come to us but there is still a level of connecting with people that maybe don't want to publish but journaling writing having a writing creative practice whatever it is is important to them stepping away having some time away in a pretty sanctuary vortex of chill kind of place is important they recognize that and understand that so rebel writers and synergy house became this space that, yes, people who are publishing and working on projects that are going to be publicly out in the world get to come into. And Rebel Writers does have the opportunity for people, if they want to contribute to one of our collaborative books as a chapter or piece, they can, but it's not a have to.

I just always felt like there was this other level of honoring what we feel so in aligned with about supporting people with their voice and just connecting to that and using writing as a tool for whatever else you're doing in the world. So those two offers, opportunities, spaces, experiences, I think supported that. So that's been something and it's still evolving and transforming and growing and telling us what it wants to be but these are the things that I wake up in the morning and I'm like, Cindy! We Voxer each other, guys. That's how we do this at the Synergy House. Synergy Publishing is a Voxer. So when you have an idea or you need to talk to somebody, that's the way we get a hold of everybody.

**Cindy:** So Shana's just picking up her Voxer to Vox Cindy, right? Diving in. So the only other thing, I think all of what Shana is saying is perfect and true and we want to honor people's words and meet them wherever they are. That has been our mission. As Shana said, from the beginning, before we even knew each other, I believe that those were our missions as Shana coached soccer, I coached cross country. In the classroom was meeting people where they are and helping them move in the direction that was already in them, is already there. We're just shining a light on it.

And the beauty of Synergy House is that I meet so many women, particularly, who are such incredible caretakers. So they're doing this creative, amazing work while having hands on everybody else in their lives. So they're writing around the edges, which I'm a big fan of. I write around the edges. I get a lot done that way. And there's something special about being taken care of for a minute instead of taking care of somebody else.

And so for me, the power in the retreat is we get to take care of you. I don't allow people to do their own dishes. Don't worry. I come home and somebody does dishes for me. It's okay. But in the retreat house, you're not allowed to. Put a dish in the sink or in the dishwasher. You leave it on the table and you want to see some women come apart. Tell them they cannot put a dish in the dishwasher. So we get comfortable being uncomfortable and we dive into that. And that's all a part of this process of feeling our way forward and finding our voices and finding our process in the way we work.

**Aimee:** Yeah, being cared for, having that space in the retreat to actually let someone else, Cindy, you cook all this yummy food and you take care of us, you feed us, and then Shana comes around us and gives us the right prompts. And you guys do kind of work in tandem to coax this book out of us using lots of different methods from breathing and moving to eating and resting. It's pretty amazing. It really is. It's a very different process and it's very female.

**Cindy:** Yeah. We have been known to say, stop writing right now, take that paddle board and go out on the lake and we'll see you in an hour.

**Shana:** Yeah. Or go take a nap.

**Aimee:** I do love a good nap. You guys, I think I did take a nap.

**Shana:** Yeah. Yeah. I mean, if people who haven't taken a nap in years. It's like, go take a nap.

**Aimee:** Yeah. You know, six kids. Oh my gosh. When I went out for my retreat, you know, I think my baby was, I don't even think he was two years old yet. And, man, it was nice to just get away and take a nap. Honestly, I think that was the first thing I did when I got to the house was actually sleep for three hours.

**Cindy:** Yes.

**Aimee:** Like, okay, let's write a book. But that's the reality. You know, the reality is we are writing around the edges. As women, we have a lot of other responsibilities. We're running companies or we have other projects. We've got kids, we've got households, you know, so we are writing. I always tell everybody, I wrote my book mostly by talking into the notes in my phone because that's when I have time to catch an idea before it flies away like a butterfly.

**Cindy:** And we love that.

**Aimee:** Yeah. So I'm now a legacy author writing my second book with you guys, The Potential Equation, which we're looking at releasing on May 1st of 2026. What does it mean to build long-term creative partnerships with your authors?

**Shana:** This is another one that came from responding. Cindy and I are both, if anyone in the audience knows human design at all, we're both generators and we can be energizer bunnies and we can just plow through some work and just put our head down. This was happening even today. And luckily we often take turns. So usually one of us is in the go mode and the other is like, are you sure? We generally aren't doing it at the same time.

But I share that to say when we started with supporting people with their words, it was a one book at a time kind of situation because that's what felt comfortable for us and what we could feel like we were in integrity to do. And almost everyone that we work with, with one book, as we're writing around the edges and whatnot, other ideas come up and they're like, I've also always thought about this. I've also always wanted to write a book of poetry. I've also got this other thing that's kind of, and so as you are a business owner and you're designing the ways you want to have containers of support, for people it didn't feel aligned and not feel fiercely aligned at all to just rinse and repeat the initial embodied writing program that a lot of people came in on.
